summary:
The adoption of Christianity by the Aksumite Kingdom (c. 330s AD) under King Ezana was one of the most consequential religious events in African history — making Aksum one of the very first states in the world to adopt Christianity as an official religion, roughly contemporaneous with Constantine's conversion in the Roman Empire. According to tradition, the Syrian-born monk Frumentius was shipwrecked as a boy on the Aksumite coast, rose to influence at the Aksumite court, was eventually consecrated as the first Bishop of Aksum by Athanasius of Alexandria, and converted King Ezana. The historical record confirms Ezana's conversion through his coinage, which shifted from pagan symbols (the crescent and disc) to the cross.

Ethiopian Christianity, rooted in this Aksumite conversion, developed into one of the world's most distinctive Christian traditions — the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, with its unique liturgy in Ge'ez (the ancient Aksumite language), its monasteries carved into sheer cliffs, its distinctive icon painting style, and its practices that preserved many Jewish elements (Sabbath observance, dietary laws, circumcision). The Nine Saints — Syrian and Egyptian monks who arrived in the 5th century — established monasteries throughout the highlands that became centers of learning and translation, producing the Ge'ez Bible. This tradition of unbroken Christian practice makes Ethiopia one of the oldest Christian nations in the world, predating the Christianization of most of Europe.
